# diagram-tour

**A Claude Code skill that turns any codebase into a narrated architecture-tour video.**

> *"explain this codebase"* → ~6 minutes of narrated MP4, walking through your project's architecture cluster by cluster, with arrows and highlights synced to the narration.

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/af7c47e5-16a2-449f-bdca-fce1b42cd079

*(A 3-minute tour of a generic Claude-style AI agent architecture, generated by this skill.)*

---

## What it does

Given a codebase, the skill orchestrates four stages:

1. **Analyze** — reads your repo's structure (READMEs, package files, top-level layout)
2. **Generate** — writes a Graphviz `.dot` file that captures the architecture at the right level of abstraction
3. **Narrate** — writes a markdown narration script following a small set of conventions, sampling source files for accuracy
4. **Render** — produces a narrated MP4 with [Piper](https://github.com/rhasspy/piper) TTS, automatic per-node arrow callouts, cluster-level highlights, smooth pan transitions, and a deterministic ffmpeg pipeline

You confirm the scope, the diagram, and the narration before any 15-minute render runs. Re-renders after narration tweaks are fast (~5 minutes) thanks to a per-sentence content-addressable voice cache.

## Install

Clone into your Claude Code skills directory and run the installer:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/<user>/diagram-tour ~/.claude/skills/diagram-tour
~/.claude/skills/diagram-tour/install.sh
```

The installer:

- Verifies system dependencies (graphviz, ffmpeg, python ≥ 3.10)
- Creates a Python venv inside the skill directory
- Installs Python deps (Pillow, PyYAML, piper-tts, pathvalidate)
- Downloads the default Piper voice model (`en_US-lessac-medium`, ~75 MB)
- Runs a smoke test

System dependencies — install via your package manager:

| OS | Command |
|---|---|
| **macOS** | `brew install graphviz ffmpeg python3` |
| **Ubuntu / Debian** | `sudo apt install graphviz ffmpeg python3 python3-venv` |
| **Fedora** | `sudo dnf install graphviz ffmpeg python3` |
| **Windows** | not directly supported — use WSL |

## Quick start

Once installed, in Claude Code:

```
/diagram-tour explain this codebase
```

The skill will confirm scope, propose a cluster shape, and walk you through the four stages with confirmation gates before each expensive step.

If you'd rather drive the pipeline directly with an existing `.dot` file and narration markdown, you can call it as a CLI:

```bash
~/.claude/skills/diagram-tour/.venv/bin/diagram-tour \
    --dot path/to/architecture.dot
```

…or with a non-default voice:

```bash
~/.claude/skills/diagram-tour/.venv/bin/diagram-tour \
    --dot path/to/architecture.dot --voice en_US-ryan-high
```

(The `python -m diagram_tour` form works too — same entry point.)

## The four stages, explained

### 1. Scope analysis

Light scan only — `README.md`, `package.json` / `pyproject.toml` / `Cargo.toml`, `ls` of `src/`. The skill proposes a cluster shape ("by top-level src/ directories"), tells you the estimated runtime, and asks for confirmation. **No expensive work yet.**

### 2. `.dot` review gate

Once scope is confirmed, the skill samples key files and writes `.diagram-tour/architecture.dot`. A preview PNG is rendered and shown. You can:

- **Approve** and continue to narration
- **Refine** in plain English: *"make it simpler"*, *"split the lib cluster"*, *"show data flow not file dependencies"*, *"focus on the API surface"*
- **Edit directly** by opening `.diagram-tour/architecture.dot` in your editor

### 3. Narration review gate

The skill reads source files referenced by node names and writes `.diagram-tour/architecture-tour.md` following the [narration conventions](./CONVENTIONS.md). You can approve, refine, or edit.

### 4. Render

The pipeline produces a versioned MP4 at `.diagram-tour/renders/<diagram>-tour-<timestamp>.mp4`, plus a `<diagram>-tour-latest.mp4` symlink. Render time is roughly proportional to diagram size:

- 10–15 nodes: ~5–7 minutes
- 30–50 nodes: ~20–30 minutes
- Heavily modulated by ffmpeg encoding speed; faster CPUs help

## Operations

The skill exposes individual stages as separate operations, useful when re-entering mid-pipeline:

| Operation | Inputs | Outputs |
|---|---|---|
| `/diagram-tour` (default) | codebase | full pipeline with confirmation gates |
| `/diagram-tour analyze` | codebase | `.dot` only, then stop |
| `/diagram-tour narrate` | existing `.dot` | rewrites `.md` against a fixed `.dot` |
| `/diagram-tour render` | `.dot` + `.md` | MP4 (no LLM, no gates) |
| `/diagram-tour preview` | `.dot` + `.md` | low-DPI render for fast iteration |

Direct CLI equivalents work too — `python -m diagram_tour --dot ...` reads the existing `.diagram-tour/` workspace.

## Configuration

Three layers, highest priority first:

1. **CLI flags** — `--voice <name>`, `--dot <path>`, `--tour <path>`
2. **Environment variables** — `DIAGRAM_TOUR_VOICE=<name>`, `PIPER_VOICES_DIR=<path>`
3. **Defaults** — `en_US-lessac-medium` voice, paths derived from `--dot`

### Swapping voices

Any voice from [rhasspy/piper-voices](https://huggingface.co/rhasspy/piper-voices) works:

```bash
# Per-invocation
python -m diagram_tour --dot foo.dot --voice en_US-ryan-high

# Per-shell
export DIAGRAM_TOUR_VOICE=en_US-ryan-high
python -m diagram_tour --dot foo.dot
```

Voices are auto-downloaded to `~/.local/share/piper-voices/` on first use. Browse samples at <https://rhasspy.github.io/piper-samples/>.

A few I've found work well for narration:

- **`en_US-ryan-high`** — measured, professional, the most natural-sounding free voice for documentary-style narration
- **`en_US-lessac-medium`** — default; recognizably TTS but clear
- **`en_GB-jenny_dioco-medium`** — distinctive English accent, very natural

Higher quality voices (`-high` tier) take ~50% longer to TTS but the difference in naturalness is substantial.

## Per-project cache

The skill keeps a `.diagram-tour/` directory at your project root (located via `.git`/`pyproject.toml`/`package.json` walk-up):

```
.diagram-tour/
├── architecture.dot                      cached, user-approved
├── architecture-tour.md                  cached, user-approved
├── architecture-hires-raw.png            graphviz output
├── architecture-hires.png                16:9-padded canvas (used by ffmpeg)
├── architecture.layout.json              cluster + node positions
├── voice-cache/<hash>.wav                per-sentence Piper output, content-addressable
└── renders/<diagram>-tour-<ts>.mp4       all versioned MP4 outputs
```

**The voice cache is the iteration win.** Per-sentence WAVs are keyed by `sha256(voice | length_scale | sentence_text)`. Edit one paragraph in your narration, re-render, and only the changed sentences re-TTS. ~5-min iteration loop instead of ~30 min.

The cache is per-project, content-addressable. Different diagrams in the same repo share the cache — boilerplate sentences ("That's the loop.", standard intros) reuse the same WAV across tours. Most savings, though, come from re-rendering the same diagram after narration edits.

Add `.diagram-tour/` to your `.gitignore` (the installer suggests this).

## Conventions

The narration follows a small set of rules so the matcher can automatically draw arrows and cluster highlights at the right moments. The full spec is in [CONVENTIONS.md](./CONVENTIONS.md), but the headline rules:

1. Use **exact node names** verbatim where natural — `triage.ts is the entry point`, not `the orchestration code is the entry point`
2. Use **exact cluster names** — `the Worker process cluster`, not `the gray box at the top`
3. List items by name, in narration order
4. Closing summaries reference clusters by name in flow order
5. Avoid pronouns that hide identity

The matcher does **exact, full-phrase matching** — no fuzzy or semantic matching. Following these conventions gives you maximum highlighting density automatically.

## How it works

Roughly:

```
.dot ──┬─→ dot -Tpng → hires PNG (raw)
       ├─→ dot -Tjson → layout JSON (cluster + node coords)
       │
       └─→ tour markdown ──┬─→ Piper TTS (per-sentence, content-cached)
                           │
                           └─→ matcher (estimates word timings, finds node + cluster mentions)
                                            │
                                            ↓
                                  PIL highlight overlays
                                            │
                                            ↓
                          ffmpeg (crop + scale + overlay chain + concat)
                                            │
                                            ↓
                                       narrated MP4
```

The `lib/` directory has one file per concern: `parse_dot.py` for layout extraction, `parse_tour_md.py` for markdown frontmatter and stop parsing, `piper_tts.py` for per-sentence TTS with explicit zero-padded silence (the bundled Piper has a buggy `--sentence-silence` flag we work around), `matcher.py` for word-timing estimation and node/cluster matching, `highlights.py` for PIL overlay rendering, `transitions.py` for ffmpeg pan-transition clips, `render.py` for static-clip rendering, `cache.py` for the per-project workspace, `voices.py` for voice resolution and auto-download.

## Project status

**v1.** Functional, used in production by the author. Open to feedback and contributions.

### Known limitations

- **Single language at a time** — works best on TypeScript/JavaScript projects with recognizable structure (Next.js, Node API, Vite). Python projects work too. Polyglot repos and large monorepos haven't been stress-tested.
- **English narration only** — Piper supports many languages but the narration conventions and matcher logic are tuned for English. PRs welcome for other languages.
- **Diagram size limits** — works comfortably up to ~50 nodes. Larger diagrams compile but become hard to read at 1280×720.
- **Windows not directly supported** — use WSL. Native Windows support would require a different Piper distribution and bash → PowerShell port of `install.sh`.
- **No fuzzy narration matching** — the matcher does exact phrase match against the narration. Common-word cluster names (e.g. a cluster literally called "Tools") can produce minor false-positive highlights when the word appears in passing. Tunable via the conventions or by renaming clusters.

## Examples

[`examples/agent-architecture/`](./examples/agent-architecture/) — a generic Claude-style AI agent architecture (External, Agent Core, Tools, Memory clusters). Includes `.dot`, narration `.md`, and the rendered MP4. Use it as a reference for your own tours.

## Contributing

Bug reports and patches welcome. The matcher logic in `lib/matcher.py` and the rendering pipeline in `render_video.py` are the most likely places to need work as new diagram patterns emerge.

When opening a PR:

- Add a fresh `.dot` example in `examples/` if your change affects rendering
- Run the full pipeline against `examples/agent-architecture/` to confirm no regression
- For convention changes, update `CONVENTIONS.md` AND the in-repo example narration
